answer.rationale: Pan-canonical lexical pattern. Mark 3:28-29 names every sin (panta ta hamartemata G3956 G265) as forgivable to the children of man (tois huiois ton anthropon G5207 G444) but blasphemes against the Holy Spirit (blasphemese eis to pneuma to hagion G987 G4151 G40) is never forgiven (ouk echei aphesin G3756 G2192 G859) and is guilty of an eternal sin (enochos estin aioniou hamartematos G1777 G166 G265). Mark 3:30 names the trigger explicitly: hoti elegon pneuma akatharton echei G3754 G2036 G4151 G169 G2192 (because they were saying he has an unclean spirit). The unforgivable speech is identified by the narrator as attributing the Spirit's exorcistic work to demonic agency. Matt 12:31 doubles the lexical force: pasa hamartia kai blasphemia G3956 G266 G988 (every sin and blasphemy) is forgiven, but he tou pneumatos blasphemia G3588 G4151 G988 (the blasphemy of the Spirit) is not forgiven (ouk aphethesetai G3756 G863). Matt 12:32 extends temporally: oute en touto to aioni oute en to mellonti G3777 G1722 G3778 G165 G3777 G1722 G3588 G3195 (neither in this age nor in the coming one). Luke 12:10 confirms with blasphemesanti eis to hagion pneuma ouk aphethesetai G987 G4151 G40. The same-domain warning texts Heb 6:4-6 (adynaton G102 to renew to repentance metanoian G3341 those who have fallen away parapesontas G3895, since they re-crucify anastaurountas G388 the Son of God) and Heb 10:26-29 (he who has trodden underfoot the Son of God and outraged the Spirit of grace enybrisas to pneuma tes charitos G1796 G4151 G5485) and 1 John 5:16 (hamartia pros thanaton G266 G4314 G2288, a sin unto death) form a coherent canonical cluster on hardened, willful, knowing rejection of the Spirit's witness to Christ. The doctrine is canonically established.
answer.notes: Two distinct questions sit inside this proposition. (1) Is there an unforgivable sin called blasphemy against the Holy Spirit? Yes, unambiguous, all three Synoptics in parallel. (2) What exactly is it? The narrator of Mark answers in 3:30: it is the settled, knowing attribution of the Spirit's salvific work to demonic agency, in the face of the Spirit's own evidential witness. The historic Christian consensus extends this contextually to final impenitence, the willful and persistent rejection of the Spirit's witness to Christ that comes to its terminus in unrepentance at death. Augustine, Aquinas, Catholic teaching, Lutheran, Reformed, Methodist, Anglican, Eastern Orthodox all converge on this narrow reading. The cult-marker bar is NOT met for this question. Denial of the proposition would be unusual and would put one outside historic Christianity, but the proposition itself is not the gospel's lexical core (deity of Christ, atonement, resurrection). Carriers of distortion to note: hyper-conscientious Christians sometimes torment themselves believing they have committed it; the pastoral consensus across all lineages is that anyone WORRIED that they have committed it has NOT committed it, because the truly hardened do not worry. Public-record carriers of denial of any unforgivable sin: classical Universalists, Unitarian Universalists, some progressive-Christian writers who treat all final-judgment language as metaphor. Carriers of confused expansion: some hyper-Pentecostal teachers who identify resistance to specific manifestation-gifts with the unforgivable sin, which the canonical text does not warrant. The question is well-attested but pastorally nuanced; lineage agreement is high on the existence of the sin and on its narrow contextual meaning.

stem_audit.notes: The question statement names the canonical category (blasphemy against the Holy Spirit, final hardened rejection of his witness to Christ, unforgivable) using the exact contextual definition the Synoptic narrators themselves supply (Mark 3:30). The parenthetical qualifier (final, hardened rejection of His witness to Christ) reflects the historic Christian reading. The proposition is testable directly against Mark 3, Matt 12, Luke 12, Heb 6, Heb 10, 1 John 5. No neutralization required.

lay_summary.reasoning: The Bible teaches there is one sin that will never be forgiven, called blasphemy against the Holy Spirit. The strongest reason is the three Gospel accounts that all say the same thing. In Mark 3:28-29 Jesus says every sin and every slander can be forgiven, but whoever slanders the Holy Spirit will never have forgiveness and is guilty of an eternal sin. Mark 3:30 then explains why he said this. The religious leaders had just said Jesus has an unclean spirit, meaning they had said the Holy Spirit's clear work of casting out demons through Jesus was actually the work of the devil. So the unforgivable sin is not a slip of the tongue, an angry word in a hard moment, or doubt during a dark time. It is a settled, knowing, deliberate refusal to admit that the Spirit's witness to Jesus is from God, even when the evidence is plain. Matthew 12:32 adds that this sin is not forgiven in this age or in the coming age. The strongest texts that look like complicating witnesses are actually in the same family. Hebrews 6:4-6 says it is impossible to renew those who have fallen away after being enlightened, because they would have to crucify the Son of God again. Hebrews 10:26-29 says if we go on sinning deliberately after receiving the truth, no sacrifice remains, and the person has outraged the Spirit of grace. 1 John 5:16-17 names a sin that leads to death, for which the apostle does not even say to pray. These texts together name the same thing the Gospels name. They warn about a hardened final rejection. The verdict handles the tension well. The sin is real and it is unforgivable, but the test for it is not a moment of fear or a bad memory of something said years ago. The test is whether the Spirit's witness to Christ has been knowingly and finally rejected. A person who is worried they have committed it has not committed it, because the truly hardened do not worry. They no longer care.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Christian lineages are essentially unanimous that there is a sin called blasphemy against the Holy Spirit and that it is unforgivable. The Apostles' Creed and the Nicene Creed do not list it explicitly but every major confession does. Patristic writers including Augustine (Sermon 71) read it as final impenitence, the willful refusal to repent that comes from persistent resistance to the Spirit's witness. Aquinas in Summa Theologica II-II Question 14 follows Augustine, defining it as final impenitence persevering in mortal sin until death. The Catechism of the Catholic Church paragraph 1864 teaches that there are no limits to God's mercy, but anyone who deliberately refuses to accept his mercy by repenting rejects the forgiveness of his sins and the salvation offered by the Holy Spirit. Lutheran teaching in the Formula of Concord names the same pattern, obstinate and persistent resistance to the Holy Spirit's work through the Word. The Anglican 39 Articles speak of sin after baptism and the gravity of final apostasy. The Methodist Articles of Religion Article XII state explicitly that not every willful sin after justification is the sin against the Holy Ghost, which guards against pastoral over-extension. Reformed traditions following Westminster Confession 17.3 treat it within perseverance, distinguishing genuine believers' falls from final apostasy. Eastern Orthodox theology reads it as final impenitence and final refusal of the Spirit's healing work. Pentecostals and Anabaptists follow the same pattern. Public-record carriers of denial are limited. Classical Universalists, some Unitarian Universalists, and certain progressive theologians who deny any final unforgivable category dissent on the existence of an unforgivable sin. Public-record carriers of confused expansion include some hyper-Pentecostal teachers who identify resistance to specific spiritual gifts with the unforgivable sin, which the canonical text does not warrant. The convergent pastoral wisdom across all lineages is that anyone worried they have committed this sin has not committed it, because the truly hardened do not worry.

scripture.key_term: πάντα τὰ ἁμαρτήματα panta ta hamartemata G3956 G265 (all the sins); τοῖς υἱοῖς τῶν ἀνθρώπων tois huiois ton anthropon G5207 G444 (to the children of man); αἱ βλασφημίαι hai blasphemiai G988 (the blasphemies)
scripture.force: Jesus issues a sweeping amnesty: all sins and all blasphemies whatever shall be forgiven to the children of man. The universal scope (panta, hosa an blasphemesosin) sets up the singular exception that follows. The text first establishes the breadth of divine forgiveness before naming the one exclusion.

scripture.key_term: βλασφημήσῃ εἰς τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον blasphemese eis to pneuma to hagion G987 G4151 G40 (blasphemes against the Holy Spirit); οὐκ ἔχει ἄφεσιν ouk echei aphesin G3756 G2192 G859 (has no forgiveness); ἔνοχός ἐστιν αἰωνίου ἁμαρτήματος enochos estin aioniou hamartematos G1777 G166 G265 (is guilty of an eternal sin)
scripture.force: The exception clause. The verb blasphemese is aorist subjunctive active, naming a definite act (not a passing thought). The phrase eis to pneuma to hagion (against the Holy Spirit) marks the target with eis of direction. The denial of forgiveness is absolute: ouk echei aphesin eis ton aiona (has no forgiveness forever). The textual variant for the final phrase has aioniou hamarteseos (eternal sin) in critical editions, naming the sin itself, not just its punishment, as eternal.

scripture.key_term: ὅτι ἔλεγον hoti elegon G3754 G2036 (because they were saying); πνεῦμα ἀκάθαρτον ἔχει pneuma akatharton echei G4151 G169 G2192 (he has an unclean spirit)
scripture.force: The narrator's own interpretive gloss. The imperfect indicative elegon (they were saying) names a settled, repeated, sustained declaration, not a one-off remark. The content of the declaration is identified: they were attributing the Spirit's exorcistic work in Jesus to an unclean spirit (pneuma akatharton). This is the decisive contextual definition of blasphemy against the Spirit: knowing, settled attribution of the Spirit's salvific work to demonic agency.

scripture.key_term: πᾶσα ἁμαρτία καὶ βλασφημία pasa hamartia kai blasphemia G3956 G266 G988 (every sin and blasphemy); ἡ τοῦ πνεύματος βλασφημία he tou pneumatos blasphemia G3588 G4151 G988 (the blasphemy of the Spirit); οὐκ ἀφεθήσεται ouk aphethesetai G3756 G863 (will not be forgiven)
scripture.force: Matthean parallel. The genitive tou pneumatos (of the Spirit) names the Spirit as the object slandered. The future passive aphethesetai with the negation ouk is decisive: divine forgiveness is withheld. The contrast pasa (every) versus he tou pneumatos (the one of the Spirit) is rhetorically deliberate.

scripture.key_term: οὔτε ἐν τούτῳ τῷ αἰῶνι οὔτε ἐν τῷ μέλλοντι oute en touto to aioni oute en to mellonti G3777 G165 G3195 (neither in this age nor in the coming one)
scripture.force: Matthew extends the denial of forgiveness temporally to both ages. The double-negation oute...oute closes the temporal door. The participle mellonti (the coming age) is a standard Jewish two-age formula. The denial of forgiveness is not merely earthly; it is eschatological.

scripture.key_term: βλασφημήσαντι εἰς τὸ ἅγιον πνεῦμα blasphemesanti eis to hagion pneuma G987 G4151 G40 (to the one having blasphemed against the Holy Spirit); οὐκ ἀφεθήσεται ouk aphethesetai G3756 G863
scripture.force: Third Synoptic parallel. The aorist participle blasphemesanti names the completed act. Luke places this saying in a different narrative context (the call to fearless confession), which shows the saying circulated as a fixed dominical word. Triple-attested in the Synoptic tradition.

scripture.key_term: ἀδύνατον adynaton G102 (impossible); παραπεσόντας parapesontas G3895 (having fallen away); ἀνακαινίζειν εἰς μετάνοιαν anakainizein eis metanoian G340 G3341 (to renew unto repentance); ἀνασταυροῦντας anastaurountas G388 (crucifying again); παραδειγματίζοντας paradeigmatizontas G3856 (publicly disgracing)
scripture.force: Same-domain warning. The author names a class of people who have been enlightened, tasted the heavenly gift, become partakers of the Holy Spirit, and tasted the powers of the coming age, then fallen away (parapesontas). For such persons it is impossible (adynaton) to renew them to repentance. The reason: they re-crucify the Son of God and put him to open shame. The lexical pattern (knowing experience of the Spirit followed by deliberate apostasy resulting in no remedy) matches the Synoptic blasphemy logion at the conceptual level.

scripture.key_term: ἑκουσίως ἁμαρτανόντων hekousios hamartanonton G1596 G264 (deliberately sinning); οὐκέτι ἀπολείπεται θυσία ouketi apoleipetai thysia G3765 G2378 (no longer remains a sacrifice); ἐνυβρίσας τὸ πνεῦμα τῆς χάριτος enybrisas to pneuma tes charitos G1796 G4151 G5485 (having outraged the Spirit of grace)
scripture.force: Same-domain warning. Deliberate (hekousios, willingly, of one's own accord) ongoing sin after receiving full knowledge of the truth (epignosis tes aletheias) leaves no remaining sacrifice for sins. The offender is named as having trampled the Son of God, profaned the blood of the covenant, and outraged the Spirit of grace (enybrisas to pneuma tes charitos). The verb enybrizo is strong; it names contemptuous, deliberate insult. The lexical cluster (deliberate, knowing, post-conversion outrage of the Spirit) mirrors the Synoptic blasphemy pattern.

scripture.key_term: ἁμαρτία πρὸς θάνατον hamartia pros thanaton G266 G4314 G2288 (a sin unto death); οὐ περὶ ἐκείνης λέγω ἵνα ἐρωτήσῃ ou peri ekeines lego hina erotese G2065 (I do not say he should ask concerning that)
scripture.force: Same-domain warning. John names a category of sin pros thanaton (toward death). For that category the apostle does not direct the praying believer to pray. The preposition pros with the accusative names tendency or trajectory: this sin leads to death. The lexical link to the Synoptic blasphemy logion is not direct, but the conceptual link (a hardened sin so settled that even intercessory prayer is not commanded) is canonically coherent. Some patristic and modern commentators identify John's hamartia pros thanaton with the Synoptic blasphemy of the Spirit.

scripture.key_term: ἐπιγνώσει epignosei G1922 (full knowledge); μιάσματα miasmata G3393 (defilements); ἡττῶνται hettontai G2274 (overcome); χείρονα ta cheirona G5501 (worse things)
scripture.force: Same-domain warning, complicating-text class. Those who have escaped the defilements of the world through full knowledge of the Lord (epignosis tou kyriou) and are again entangled and overcome end up worse off than before. The lexical motif of post-knowledge return to defilement matches the Heb 6 and Heb 10 cluster. The text complicates and extends the canonical picture: hardened apostasy after enlightenment is a real possibility with no remedy.

scripture.key_term: ἐψεύσασθαι τὸ πνεῦμα τὸ ἅγιον epseusasthai to pneuma to hagion G5574 G4151 G40 (to lie to the Holy Spirit); πειράσαι τὸ πνεῦμα κυρίου peirasai to pneuma kyriou G3985 G4151 G2962 (to test the Spirit of the Lord)
scripture.force: Ananias and Sapphira are named by Peter as having lied to and tested the Holy Spirit. Both are struck dead. The narrative establishes a New Testament category of deliberate, knowing offense against the Spirit himself (not merely against the church or against an apostle) with immediate divine judgment. The lexical pattern (deliberate, knowing offense against the person of the Spirit) reinforces that the Spirit is a divine person who can be sinned against in a distinct and grave way.

scripture.key_term: σήμερον semeron G4594 (today); σκληρύνητε τὰς καρδίας ὑμῶν sklerynete tas kardias hymon G4645 G2588 (harden your hearts); ἀποστῆναι ἀπὸ θεοῦ ζῶντος apostenai apo theou zontos G868 G2316 G2198 (to fall away from the living God)
scripture.force: Citing Ps 95, the author warns against hardening the heart against the Holy Spirit's voice today. The conceptual frame (resisting the Spirit's witness leading to apostasy from the living God) is the canonical horizon within which the blasphemy logion operates. The Spirit's witness is here, calling, today; persistent hardening is the doorway to apostasy.

scripture.key_term: ἀντιπίπτετε τῷ πνεύματι τῷ ἁγίῳ antipiptete to pneumati to hagio G496 G4151 G40 (you resist the Holy Spirit)
scripture.force: Stephen's accusation of his hearers. The verb antipipto (to fall against, resist) in the present indicative names a settled, ongoing posture of resistance to the Spirit. The lexical pattern (settled, willful resistance to the Spirit's witness through the prophets and through Christ) is the same shape as the Synoptic blasphemy logion. Stephen's audience is not yet beyond reach (some, like Saul, will repent), but the pattern of willful, settled resistance is named with the same precision as the Gospels.

scripture.key_term: ἀγνοῶν ἐποίησα ἐν ἀπιστίᾳ agnoon epoiesa en apistia G50 G4160 G570 (acting in ignorance, in unbelief); ἠλεήθην eleethen G1653 (I obtained mercy)
scripture.force: Critical Pauline self-disclosure. Paul confesses he was formerly a blasphemer (blasphemos G989), persecutor, and insolent man, yet he received mercy because he acted in ignorance and in unbelief. The implicit logic: Paul's pre-conversion blasphemies were NOT the unforgivable blasphemy of the Spirit, because they were ignorant, not knowing-and-settled rejection of the Spirit's witness. This text guards against pastoral over-extension: not every act of blasphemy is the blasphemy of the Spirit.

scripture.key_term: ἐν τῷ Βεελζεβοὺλ ἄρχοντι τῶν δαιμονίων ἐκβάλλει en to Beelzeboul archonti ton daimonion ekballei G954 G758 G1140 G1544 (he casts out demons by Beelzebul, the prince of demons)
scripture.force: The immediate narrative trigger for the blasphemy logion in both Matthew and Mark. The Pharisees say Jesus' exorcisms (manifest works of the Spirit) are powered by Satan. This is the specific accusation that earns the unforgivable-sin warning. The lexical context defines what blasphemy of the Spirit IS in canonical-narrative terms: the deliberate attribution of the Spirit's clear messianic work to demonic agency.

hermeneutics.notes: Grammatico-historical reading of the triple-Synoptic logion is decisive. The Markan narrator's own gloss in 3:30 supplies the contextual definition: blasphemy of the Spirit is the settled, knowing attribution of the Spirit's clear salvific work to demonic agency. No interpretive framework (covenantal, dispensational, new-covenant, progressive-covenantalism) produces a different verdict at the level of the sin's existence. Frameworks may differ on adjacent questions (eternal security, the temporal possibility of post-conversion apostasy) but the existence and identity of the unforgivable sin is invariant. Analogia scripturae is invoked: Heb 6:4-6, Heb 10:26-29, 1 John 5:16-17, 2 Pet 2:20-22 form a coherent canonical cluster on hardened final apostasy. The 1 Tim 1:13 complicating text disciplines the reading: Paul's pre-conversion blasphemies were not the unforgivable sin because they were ignorant, not knowing-and-settled. This guards against pastoral over-extension. The Acts 5 Ananias narrative confirms the Spirit can be sinned against personally and gravely. Progressive revelation is not a major factor; the logion is dominical and the same shape appears across Synoptics, Hebrews, and Johannine corpus.

counter_witness.anchor: Augustine, Sermon 71 on the unpardonable sin against the Holy Ghost, NPNF1 Vol 6
counter_witness.key_phrase: The sin against the Holy Ghost is final impenitence. He who continues to the end in the obstinacy of evil will, against the witness of the Spirit, has no forgiveness.

counter_witness.anchor: Aquinas, Summa Theologica II-II Question 14 On the Sin Against the Holy Ghost, ccel.org/ccel/aquinas/summa/SS/SS014.html
counter_witness.key_phrase: Blasphemy or the sin against the Holy Ghost, is final impenitence when a man perseveres in mortal sin until death.

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church paragraph 1864, vatican.va/archive/ENG0015/__P6C.HTM
counter_witness.key_phrase: There are no limits to the mercy of God, but anyone who deliberately refuses to accept his mercy by repenting, rejects the forgiveness of his sins and the salvation offered by the Holy Spirit.

counter_witness.anchor: Formula of Concord, Solid Declaration Article II On Free Will, bookofconcord.org/solid-declaration/free-will/
counter_witness.key_phrase: All those who obstinately and persistently resist the operations and movements of the Holy Ghost, which take place through the Word, do not receive, but grieve and lose, the Holy Ghost.

counter_witness.anchor: Thirty-Nine Articles of Religion (1571), Article XVI Of Sin after Baptism, churchofengland.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: Not every deadly sin willingly committed after Baptism is sin against the Holy Ghost, and unpardonable. Wherefore the grant of repentance is not to be denied to such as fall into sin after Baptism.

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith Chapter 17 Section 3 On Perseverance
counter_witness.key_phrase: True believers may fall into grievous sins and incur God's displeasure, but final apostasy and the sin against the Holy Ghost remain warnings against hardened impenitence.

counter_witness.anchor: Methodist Articles of Religion (Wesley's abridgement of the 39 Articles), Article XII Of Sin After Justification, umc.org/en/content/articles-of-religion
counter_witness.key_phrase: Not every sin willingly committed after justification is the sin against the Holy Ghost, and unpardonable. The grant of repentance is not to be denied to such as fall into sin after justification.

counter_witness.anchor: Dordrecht Confession of Faith (1632), on repentance and final apostasy
counter_witness.key_phrase: We confess that the falling away into final impenitence and hardened rejection of the Spirit's witness is a grave warning held forth in Scripture.

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God doctrinal teaching on the unpardonable sin (pastoral materials referenced from ag.org)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Blasphemy of the Holy Spirit is willful, malicious, persistent attribution of the Spirit's work to Satan; a person worried about having committed it has not committed it.

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox theology of final impenitence and resistance to the Spirit's healing work, OCA teaching materials
counter_witness.key_phrase: The blasphemy of the Holy Spirit is the persistent, final refusal of the Spirit's healing and converting grace, the willed rejection that closes the door against mercy.

web.quote: Mark 3:29 blasphemese eis to pneuma to hagion (G987 G4151 G40); ouk echei aphesin (G3756 G2192 G859); enochos aioniou hamartematos (G166 G265).
Mark 3:30 hoti elegon pneuma akatharton echei (G3754 G2036 G4151 G169 G2192). Narrator's gloss naming what the blasphemy was.
Matt 12:31 pasa hamartia kai blasphemia (G3956 G266 G988); he tou pneumatos blasphemia ouk aphethesetai (G4151 G988 G3756 G863).
Matt 12:32 oute en touto to aioni oute en to mellonti (G3777 G165 G3195). Double-negation closes the temporal door across both ages.
Luke 12:10 blasphemesanti eis to hagion pneuma ouk aphethesetai (G987 G4151 G40 G3756 G863). Third Synoptic parallel.
G988 blasphemia: vilification, especially against God; HELPS notes the term switches right for wrong, exchanging God's truth for a lie. 18 NT occurrences.
G987 blasphemeo: to speak reproachfully or irreverently of God or sacred things. 35 NT occurrences. Refusing to acknowledge what is truly worthy of reverence.
Heb 6:6 parapesontas (G3895) having fallen away; adynaton anakainizein eis metanoian (G102 G340 G3341); anastaurountas (G388) crucifying again.
Heb 10:26 hekousios hamartanonton (G1596 G264) deliberately sinning; ouketi apoleipetai thysia (G3765 G2378). v.29 enybrisas to pneuma tes charitos (G1796).
1 John 5:16 hamartia pros thanaton (G266 G4314 G2288). John does not say to pray for this category of sin. Same-domain warning text.
CCC 1864: no limits to God's mercy, but anyone who deliberately refuses to accept his mercy by repenting rejects the forgiveness of his sins offered by the Holy Spirit.
Formula of Concord SD II: all those who obstinately and persistently resist the operations of the Holy Ghost through the Word grieve and lose the Holy Ghost.
Methodist Article XII: not every sin willingly committed after justification is the sin against the Holy Ghost and unpardonable; repentance remains available.
